one_line_takeaway: JensUn replaces unbounded log-likelihood/KL unlearning losses with a bounded Jensen-Shannon-Divergence objective on both forget and retain sets, achieving near-zero worst-case forget accuracy with minimal utility loss and strong resistance to benign relearning - while the paper's harder LLM-judge + worst-case evaluation shows prior methods were substantially overrated.
key_finding: On LKF with Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct (Table 1), JensUn reaches 0.0 worst-case forget accuracy (J_W) while preserving utility best: retain J_Avg 52.3 (vs original 52.6), MMLU 59.9 (vs 59.6 original), and the highest response quality among effective methods (WR 0.47, tied with RMU, vs base 0.5). GradAscent and GradDiff also drive forget accuracy near 0 but wreck utility (GradAscent WR=0, MMLU 23.4; GradDiff WR=0.22, Rep 442). On RWKU (Table 2) JensUn gives the lowest forget accuracy on both subsets (FB J_W 16.3, QA J_W 6.1 vs base Phi-3 91.0/78.6) while keeping MMLU 63.2 (base 63.4) and the best WR 0.52; its nearest competitor GradDiff is ~16 points worse on QA forget accuracy. The evaluation-critique finding: worst-case evaluation exposes large overestimation of prior unlearning quality - forget accuracy rises ~31% for the original Llama on LKF and up to 29% across unlearned models, and +17% for base Phi-3 (6-28% across methods) on RWKU, versus single-query evaluation. Robustness finding (Table 3): stronger/longer unlearning makes benign relearning harder; JensUn after 2000 unlearning steps resists relearning (relearnt J_W 8.0) while keeping WR 0.39-0.46, whereas NPO/NPO+SAM stay vulnerable (relearnt forget accuracy 26%/58%) with poor WR, and GradDiff only resists relearning because it is already broken (WR 0.03).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is JensUn, an LLM unlearning method that uses the Jensen-Shannon Divergence (JSD) as the training objective for BOTH the forget-set loss (minimizing JSD between the model's output distribution and a fixed target string such as the refusal 'No idea') and the retain-set loss (minimizing JSD between the unlearned and reference-model distributions), plugged into the standard L = lambda_F * L_F + lambda_R * L_R unlearning framework (Eq. 6). The authors argue JSD's key advantage over the log-likelihood losses used by GradAscent/GradDiff and over KL is that it is bounded above and below (0 <= L_F^JSD <= |y_target| log 2), symmetric, and well-defined on the union of supports, so training does not diverge arbitrarily and can be run for longer without destroying utility. Two further, SEPARATE contributions are (1) a new evaluation framework replacing ROUGE with an LLM semantic judge (Gemini-2.5-Flash) and adding a sample-wise WORST-CASE forget metric over paraphrases (J_P), in-context retain demonstrations (J_ICR), and their combination (J_W); and (2) LKF (Lesser-Known Facts), a new unlearning dataset. Note: GradAscent, GradDiff, DPO, NPO, SimNPO, RMU, RT, ICU, and NPO+SAM are cited PRIOR methods used as baselines, not this paper's contributions.
threat_model: Genuine safety/security framing set by the paper itself: LLMs trained on scraped data acquire private information, copyrighted content, and harmful knowledge; unlearning aims to remove such information post-training (right-to-be-forgotten, deletion of private/sensitive or dangerous knowledge) more cheaply than retraining. The central adversarial concern the paper attacks is that existing unlearning only SUPPRESSES rather than eradicates knowledge: (a) knowledge can be re-elicited by paraphrasing the query or adding in-context examples (evaluation-gaming / prompt-format attack surface), and (b) 'benign relearning', where fine-tuning the unlearned model on data disjoint from the forget set recovers the supposedly forgotten information. The paper measures resilience against this benign-relearning recovery rather than proposing an unlearning attack.
limitations: Evaluated models are small (3-3.8B: Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, Phi-3 Mini-4K-Instruct); no results on larger LLMs. The LLM-Judge and paraphrase/relearning pipeline depend on external judge models (Gemini-2.5-Flash) and on the quality of generated paraphrases. LKF is deliberately narrow (100 forget / 400 retain QA pairs over five niche historical topics), a proxy for private-info removal rather than actual private data. RWKU retain scores are depressed because the training retain set differs from the evaluation one, complicating cross-method retain comparison. Robustness is shown only against 'benign' relearning on disjoint data, not against adversarial/targeted relearning attacks; and the finding that longer unlearning resists relearning is presented as a hypothesis (moving further from the pretrained state) rather than proven. Forget accuracy is never perfectly zero on RWKU (residual 6.1-16.3%).

datasets_used: LKF (Lesser-Known Facts) - authors' new benchmark: 100 forget + 400 retain QA pairs across 5 niche historical topics (Challenger Disaster, Salem Witch Trials, Cod Wars, 1883 Krakatoa eruption, Battle of Talas); non-dichotomous questions
RWKU (Real-World Knowledge Unlearning) - existing benchmark, 10-target batch setting, FB and QA subsets
AlpacaEval instructions (used for repetitiveness and Win-Rate response-quality generations)
Human-labeled set of 400 queries (for ROUGE-vs-LLM-Judge ROC validation)
quantitative_results: LKF / Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct (Table 1): JensUn J_W (worst-case forget acc) = 0.0, retain J_Avg = 52.3, MMLU = 59.9, Repetitiveness = 592, WR = 0.47
LKF / Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct original model (Table 1): forget J_W = 76.0, retain J_Avg = 52.6, MMLU = 59.6, Rep = 637, WR = 0.5

LKF (Table 1) GradAscent J_W=0.0 but MMLU=23.4, Rep=0.0, WR=0; GradDiff J_W=2.0, WR=0.22; DPO J_W=32.0, WR=0.42; NPO J_W=6.0 but retain J_Avg=16.0, WR=0.27; RMU J_W=19.0, WR=0.47; SimNPO J_W=32.0, WR=0.10
RWKU / Phi-3-Mini-4K (Table 2): JensUn FB J_W = 16.3, QA J_W = 6.1, retain FB J_Avg = 40.8, QA J_Avg = 42.4, MMLU = 63.2, Rep = 694, WR = 0.52
RWKU base Phi-3-Mini-4K (Table 2): FB J_W = 91.0, QA J_W = 78.6, MMLU = 63.4, Rep = 708, WR = 0.5; nearest competitor GradDiff FB J_W=22.3, QA J_W=22.1, WR=0.42

Worst-case vs single-query forget-accuracy inflation: +31% for original Llama on LKF (up to +29% across unlearned models); +17% for base Phi-3 on RWKU (6-28% across methods) with N_P = 15 (LKF) / 9 (RWKU) paraphrases

Benign relearning (Table 3, LKF): JensUn at 2000 unlearning steps has relearnt J_W = 8.0 with WR 0.39 (WR 0.46 at 1000 steps); NPO relearnt J_W = 26.0 (WR 0.15) and NPO+SAM relearnt J_W = 58.0 (WR 0.1) at 2000 steps; GradDiff relearnt J_W=0.0 but WR collapses to 0.03
baselines_compared: Evaluation baseline: ROUGE-L (replaced by LLM-Judge Gemini-2.5-Flash)
